title: Stolen-Credential SSH Login
briefing: A Linux host, $TARGET, recorded a SUCCESSFUL SSH login (Accepted password) for the account $USER1 from external source $SRC1 — with no failed attempts before it. This is not a brute force: someone logged straight in with valid credentials. Decide whether this is normal admin activity or an adversary using stolen creds, and justify it from the evidence.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 5715 (sshd authentication success) or rule.groups: authentication_success. Look at data.srcip and data.dstuser. The tell is what is MISSING: no 5710/5716 failures precede the success, and the source IP is external/unfamiliar. Successful auth from an odd source with zero failures = valid-account abuse, not brute force.

options: Valid-account abuse — successful login with stolen/guessed credentials
questions.answer: Valid-account abuse — successful login with stolen/guessed credentials
questions.explain: A clean successful auth (rule 5715) from an unfamiliar external IP, with no preceding failures, is credential abuse — the attacker already had working creds.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: Why is this HARDER to catch than a brute force?
options: No failed logins are generated — it does not trip failure/lockout thresholds
questions.answer: No failed logins are generated — it does not trip failure/lockout thresholds
questions.explain: Brute force lights up failure counters; a valid-cred login produces a single success event, so failure-based detections and lockouts never fire.
Successful auth with legitimate credentials = T1078; there is no brute force (no failures) and no exploit.

questions.prompt: First response actions (select all that apply):
options: Reset $USER1's password and revoke its SSH keys
Hunt $TARGET for post-login activity (sudo, new users, keys, cron)
Do nothing — a successful login is normal
answer: Reset $USER1's password and revoke its SSH keys
Hunt $TARGET for post-login activity (sudo, new users, keys, cron)
questions.explain: Assume the creds are compromised: rotate them, terminate sessions, block the source, and treat $TARGET as potentially breached — hunt for what they did after logging in.
